writer fills empty target with source on bridge side; native preserves empty target

native bug #504 — the native archive reader extracts ZIP
entries line-by-line (one Block per non-empty line of raw
XML) instead of dispatching through subfilters. Bridge
correctly emits ~3 translatable Blocks from the XLIFF
(skipping the translate="no" trans-unit) plus the TMX
source segments; native emits ~56 Blocks of raw XML markup.
Tracked separately as a native architectural bug.

native bug #504 — the native reader emits ~56 raw-XML
Blocks instead of the bridge's ~3 translatable Blocks; the
inventory it would re-extract on a second pass is its own
line-by-line output, not the subfilter contract. Tracked
separately as a native architectural bug.

native bug #504 — native default `filePatterns` includes
`*.txt`, so the reader extracts `stuff.txt` line-by-line
and emits one Block ("stuff not to extract") instead of
zero. Bridge defaults to `*.tmx,*.xlf,*.xlff` which
excludes `.txt` — the contract Okapi documents. Tracked
separately as a native architectural bug (default-pattern
surface mismatch is one symptom).

native bug #504 — same root cause as the other examples:
the native reader extracts entry contents line-by-line
instead of dispatching to subfilters. Bridge enumerates
two entries (`SF-12-Test02.xlf`, `test1.tmx`), dispatches
each through its configured subfilter, and emits ~2
translatable Blocks; native emits ~49 raw-XML Blocks.
Tracked separately as a native architectural bug.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row in addition to data rows, yielding 4 Blocks ("Col1",
"R1", "R2", "R3") instead of native's 3 (header skipped). Spec
asserts native (skip-header) behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi
okf_commaseparatedvalues defaults to one-TextUnit-per-row,
extracting only column 1 (1-based, via sourceColumns="1") and
INCLUDING the header row. Bridge yields 3 Blocks ("name",
"Widget", "Gadget") instead of native's 6 Blocks (every cell
across both data rows). Spec asserts native (cell-per-Block,
skip-header) behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — same as
three_columns_two_rows: Okapi yields one Block per row from
column 1 only (4 Blocks: header + 3 data rows). Native yields
one Block per cell across all 7 columns × 3 data rows = 21
Blocks. Spec asserts native behaviour.

bridge config divergence — neokapi config key
`translatableColumns` is not recognised by the upstream Okapi
filter (which uses `sourceColumns` as a comma-separated
string of 1-based indices); the bridge therefore extracts
every column, yielding 6 Blocks (Source text 1 / Target text
1 / third column data1 / Source text 2 / Target text 2 /
third column data2). Native correctly restricts extraction
to column 0 and yields 2 Blocks. Tracked under #530 (config-key alias rollout).

bridge config divergence — same as `source_only_three_column`:
the bridge does not recognise `translatableColumns`, falls
back to extracting every column, and yields 10 Blocks
(every source + target + data cell from both rows). Native
correctly extracts only columns 0 and 3 and yields 4 Blocks.
Tracked under #530 (config-key alias rollout).

bridge config divergence — neokapi config keys
`translatableColumns` and `commentColumns` are not recognised
by the upstream Okapi filter (which uses `sourceColumns` and
`commentColumns` with 1-based indices and a comma-separated
string format); the bridge falls back to extracting every
column. Native correctly extracts columns 0 and 2 and
attaches column 1 as comment metadata, yielding 4 Blocks.
Tracked under #530 (config-key alias rollout).

bridge config divergence — same as `tab_delimiter`: the
neokapi `separator` key is not recognised by the upstream
Okapi filter (which expects `fieldDelimiter`), so the bridge
falls back to `,` and reads each `;`-joined row as a single
column. Native correctly reads three `;`-delimited columns
and yields 3 Blocks. Tracked under #530 (config-key alias rollout).

bridge config divergence — neokapi config key `separator` is
not recognised by the upstream Okapi filter (which expects
`fieldDelimiter`); the bridge therefore reads the input with
the default `,` delimiter, treats the entire `name\tdescription`
row as a single header column, and yields one Block per data
row instead of one Block per cell. Native correctly reads two
tab-delimited columns and yields 2 Blocks. Tracked under #530
(config-key alias rollout).

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row, yielding 2 Blocks ("name", "Widget") versus
native's 1 ("Widget", header skipped). Both wrap the document
in StartDocument/EndDocument event pairs; they disagree on
whether the header text is translatable. Spec asserts native
(skip-header) behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row even when no data rows follow, yielding 1 Block
("Col1") versus native's 0 (header skipped, no data rows). Both
wrap the document in document-start/end events; they disagree
on whether a lone header line is translatable. Spec asserts
native behaviour.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row and only column 1 by default; bridge yields 2
Blocks ("Col1", "has\nnewline") versus native's 2 ("has\nnewline",
"normal"). Both honor the RFC 4180 §2.6 contract that the LF
inside the quoted field is part of the value; they disagree
on column selection. Spec asserts native behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row and only column 1 by default, yielding 3 Blocks
("Col1", "R1C1", "R3C1") versus native's 4 (every non-empty
cell across both data rows). Spec asserts native behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts only
column 1 (sourceColumns default), so the empty col-1 cell in
row 2 yields no Block; bridge yields 2 Blocks ("Col1", "R1C1")
versus native's 3 ("R1C1", "R1C3", "R2C2"). Both honor the
empty-cell-skipped contract; they disagree on which columns
are translatable. Spec asserts native (all-columns) behaviour.

bridge config divergence — same as `single_key_column`:
`translatableColumns` and `keyColumns` are not recognised by
the upstream Okapi filter, so the bridge extracts every cell.
Native correctly composes Block IDs from columns 0+1 and
extracts only column 2, yielding 2 Blocks ("Save", "Cancel").
Tracked under #530 (config-key alias rollout).

bridge config divergence — neokapi config keys
`translatableColumns` and `keyColumns` are not recognised by
the upstream Okapi filter (which uses `sourceColumns` and
`sourceIdColumns`); the bridge falls back to extracting every
column, so the id column values (01, 02, 03) appear as
Blocks alongside the value column. Native correctly restricts
extraction to column 1 and uses column 0 for the Block ID,
yielding 3 Blocks. Tracked under #530 (config-key alias rollout).

bridge config divergence — neokapi config key `hasHeader` is
not recognised by the upstream Okapi filter (which uses
`columnNamesLineNum=0` to mean "no header"); the bridge
therefore keeps the default `columnNamesLineNum=1` and treats
row 1 (`Widget,A useful widget`) as the header row, yielding
only 2 Blocks (Gadget, A cool gadget). Native correctly
extracts all 4 cells as Blocks. Tracked under #530 (config-key alias rollout).

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row and only column 1 by default, yielding 2 Blocks
("Col1", "has,comma") versus native's 2 cells from the data
row ("has,comma", "normal"). Both honor the RFC 4180 §2.5
contract that the embedded comma is part of the value (not a
delimiter); they disagree on which columns are translatable.
Spec asserts native (all-columns) behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi yields 2
Blocks ("Col1", `says "hi"`) — header + column 1 of row 1
versus native's 2 ("says \"hi\"", "normal") — both cells of
row 1. Both honor the RFC 4180 §2.7 doubled-quote decoding
contract; they disagree on column selection. Spec asserts
native (all-columns, skip-header) behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row + only column 1, yielding 3 Blocks ("key",
"greeting", "farewell"). The CJK text in column 2 never
surfaces. Native extracts every cell → 4 Blocks including
"你好世界" and "さようなら". Both honor UTF-8 verbatim
preservation; they disagree on column selection. Spec asserts
native (all-columns) behaviour.

bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row + only column 1, yielding 2 Blocks ("Col1",
"R1C1"). Native skips the header and extracts both data cells
→ 2 Blocks ("R1C1", "R1C2"). Both correctly strip the UTF-8
BOM from the first cell name; they disagree on column
selection / header handling. Spec asserts native behaviour.

bridge produces leading space — Okapi DoxygenFilter preserves
the whitespace after the `\brief` command marker that the
native reader trims. Spec asserts native (trimmed) behavior.

bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
treats the orphaned `</summary>` end tag as a Block boundary,
splitting the comment text into two Blocks where native folds
it into one (HTML inline markup pass-through). Both
legitimate; spec asserts native (single-block) behavior.

bridge bug — Okapi DoxygenFilter splits the surrounding prose
into two Blocks instead of folding it into one and may extract
the `\image` argument despite the spec contract that `\image`
arguments (filenames, rendering hints) are non-translatable.
Native correctly skips. Tracked separately as a bridge
image-command-handling bug.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts 3 Blocks from the qt-style header where native folds
adjacent comment runs into 2. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native (folded) behavior.

bridge produces leading space — Okapi DoxygenFilter preserves
the whitespace after the `\return` command marker that the
native reader trims. Spec asserts native (trimmed) behavior.

bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts 8 Blocks across the sample header where native
extracts 7. The has_block_with_text / no_block_with_text
assertions still match in spirit (no untranslatable content
leaks into bridge output), the divergence is purely in
paragraph segmentation. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native (folded) behavior.

bridge produces leading space — Okapi DoxygenFilter preserves
the whitespace after the `\note` / `\warning` command markers
that the native reader trims. Spec asserts native (trimmed)
behavior on both blocks.

bridge bug — Okapi DoxygenFilter extracts 10 Blocks instead of
7 from the special-commands header AND extracts the verbatim
block content ("This text is not translated") as a Block,
despite the spec contract that `\verbatim … \endverbatim` is
non-translatable. Native correctly skips. Same root cause as
`verbatim_block_excluded/verbatim_between_prose`. Tracked
separately as a bridge verbatim-handling bug.

bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts the trailing details line ("Some details about the
Test class") as a separate Block, where the spec folds it
into the brief description's surroundings. Both legitimate
interpretations of the comment block; spec asserts native
(single-block) behavior.

bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts an empty Block for the bare `///<` marker where the
spec contract (and native reader) treat an empty trailing
comment as no extractable content. Spec asserts native
behavior; the divergence is whether an empty-content marker
should still emit an envelope-only Block.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

bridge bug — Okapi DoxygenFilter extracts the verbatim block
content ("not translated") as a translatable Block, despite
the spec contract that `\verbatim … \endverbatim` content is
non-translatable. Native correctly skips. Tracked separately
as a bridge verbatim-handling bug.

bridge reifies entity references as inline Codes (per Okapi
DTDFilter design — see feature description), so the raw
`&ent1;` / `%pent1;` tokens are stripped from the source
text and only "=ent1, =pent1" remains. Native keeps the
raw tokens in the source. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior.

bridge reifies the predefined XML entities (`&` `<`
`>`) as inline Codes rather than decoding them inline,
so the resulting source text omits `&` and `<...>`. Native
decodes them to literal characters per the Okapi
DTDFilterTest#testDoubleExtraction contract. Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — when the only declared column
starts past the actual line length, the upstream Okapi
FixedWidthColumnsFilter falls back to extracting the
whole line as a single Block ("Short"), producing 1
Block. Native correctly skips the column (start beyond
line length → no extractable text → no Block) and
produces 0 Blocks. Both honor the rune-level extraction
contract for in-range columns; they disagree on the
out-of-range fallback. Spec asserts native (skip the
column) behavior.

bridge preserves both whitespace runs surrounding the
comment placeholder ("Before  after." with double space)
while native collapses adjacent whitespace into a single
space ("Before after."). Both legitimate; spec asserts
native (HTML5 whitespace-collapse) behavior.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

bridge preserves whitespace surrounding the translate="no"
span placeholder, yielding "text with a  and more." with
double space. Native collapses adjacent whitespace per
HTML5 whitespace handling. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior.

bridge bug (#506) — Okapi ICMLFilter extracts <Note> content
as a translatable block even when extractNotes is false (the
schema-documented default), and additionally splits the
surrounding text at the Note boundary so the trailing
Content run is dropped. Native correctly skips the Note
subtree and joins the surrounding text into one Block.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

bridge default config descends into HTML blocks (extracts
"HTML content" as a third translatable Block) instead of
treating them as opaque Data. Native skips HTML blocks
when no htmlSubfilter is configured. Both legitimate;
spec asserts native behavior. Could be reconciled by
explicitly disabling HTML extraction in the bridge config.

bridge segments indented code blocks at finer granularity
than native — Okapi MarkdownFilter surfaces the indented
code block as a third Block (visible to the spec) rather
than skipping it as Data. Native skips per
translateCodeBlocks=false default. Both legitimate;
spec asserts native behavior.

bridge default config strips autolink URLs from the source
text (treats `<url>` as inline code with no translatable
content), yielding "Visit  now". Native keeps the URL as
part of the source text per CommonMark autolink semantics.
Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge bug — same soft-break handling as emphasis_across_soft_break.
Inside a list item, Okapi MarkdownFilter preserves the literal `\n`
("First\nelement") instead of collapsing it to a space. Native
collapses per CommonMark. Tracked separately as a bridge soft-break
handling bug.

bridge bug (#509) — Okapi MIFFilter does not expose
`<Char Tab>` as a literal tab (U+0009) in the source
text; the two flanking `<String>` runs come through
joined with nothing between them. Native decodes the
tab inline per the Adobe MIF Reference. The text
flanking the tab still surfaces on both sides; the
missing `\t` is the divergence captured here.

native reader does not parse Moses InlineText `<g>` markup —
upstream Okapi MosesTextFilter (testCode2) extracts only the
surrounding text and emits `<g>` as inline Codes. Native
treats `<g id="2">Text</g> <x id="1"/>` as literal text in
the Block source. Tracked as a native-reader follow-up.

native reader does not decode Moses InlineText `<lb/>` —
upstream Okapi MosesTextFilter (testFromFile, Test01/Test02
fixtures) decodes `<lb/>` to a literal newline inside the
Block source. Native preserves the literal `<lb/>` markup.
Tracked as a native-reader follow-up.

native reader does not parse `<mrk mtype="seg">` annotations
— upstream MosesTextFilter (testWhiteSpaces) extracts only
the body and emits the `<mrk>` tags as skeleton. Native
emits the full line literally. Tracked as a native-reader
follow-up.

native reader does not decode XML entities — upstream
MosesTextFilter (testLiterals) decodes `<`/`>`/`&`
to `<`/`>`/`&`. Native preserves the literal `<` etc.
Tracked as a native-reader follow-up.

neokapi extracts slide masters and speaker notes from hidden
slides even when translateSlideMasters=false and
translateHiddenSlides=false. Native defaults disagree with
the Okapi contract; tracked here until the reader honors the
flags consistently.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently
bridge error: drain stream: daemon: Error opening zipped input file.

native bug — the regex `\(([^)]*)\)\s*Tj` in
core/formats/pdf/reader.go does not allow escaped `\)` in
the capture group, so a string like `(Cost: \(US\$50\) \\
\101OK)` never matches and the block is dropped entirely
(zero blocks extracted). PDFBox correctly decodes the
string per ISO 32000-1 §7.3.4.2. Tracked as #510 (native
bug filed alongside this spec) — fixing it requires
replacing the regex tokenizer with a small PDF
string-literal parser.

native bug — the regex content-stream walker in
core/formats/pdf/reader.go cannot decode glyph-index strings
via a font's ToUnicode CMap (it has no font handling at
all), so this 11-page paper extracts zero Blocks instead of
the body text the bridge recovers. Tracked as #510 (native
bug filed alongside this spec) along with the escape-paren
gap. PDFBox handles both correctly.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

bridge bug — Okapi PHPContentFilter does not decode the
`\'` escape inside single-quoted strings, yielding
"It\'s a test" instead of "It's a test". Per the PHP
Manual ("Single quoted") only `\'` and `\\` are valid
inside single-quoted strings and both should decode to
their literal character. Native correctly decodes per
spec. Tracked as neokapi/neokapi#498.

bridge default config does not enable Okapi's inline-code
finder for the PHP filter, so `$name` / `$app` stay in the
literal source text ("Hello $name, welcome to $app").
Native applies its built-in PHP-variable detector
unconditionally and exposes the variables as inline codes,
yielding "Hello , welcome to ". Both legitimate; spec
asserts native behavior. Could be reconciled by enabling
`inlineCodes.enabled` in the bridge config (the inline
codes block is reachable in the bridge schema only via a
nested `$ref` so the spec doesn't expose it as a flat key
today — same situation as Properties / PO).
bridge error: drain stream: daemon: The encoding 'ENCODING' is not supported.

bridge default config strips printf placeholders (`%s`,
`%d`, `%f`) from the source text via Okapi's default
code-finder rules, yielding "Text  and  and ". Native
treats them as literal text under the standard PO
config. Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

Native reader does not yet act on bilingualMode=false; it
continues to treat msgid as source. Okapi's monolingual
configuration uses msgstr as source. Update reader to
honor the flag, then drop this expected_fail.

Native reader exposes 2 plural forms (singular + plural)
regardless of nplurals; Okapi's testThreePlurals expects
one text unit per msgstr[N]. Update reader to enumerate
all msgstr[N] entries, then drop this expected_fail.

bridge bug — Okapi PropertiesFilter loses the literal `\r`
escape (returns empty in its place) and double-escapes the
trailing `\\`, producing "Text1\n=lf, \t=tab, \\w=w, =cr,
\\\\=bs". Native correctly decodes per Java property spec
to "Text1\n=lf, \t=tab, \\w=w, \r=cr, \\=bs". Tracked
separately as a bridge escape-decoding bug.

Native bufio reader splits on `\n` only, so `\r` lines
collapse into a single logical line. Okapi yields two
entries; native yields one. Tracked.
Same divergence as skip_single_entry — directives ignored.

Native reader does not honour Okapi localization
directives. Tracked until useLd support lands; until then
the spec asserts the Okapi-correct outcome and the native
runner records expected_fail.

bridge bug — with useJavaEscapes off, Okapi PropertiesFilter
keeps the `\\` doubled instead of decoding it once, yielding
"...a \\!\\= b \\\\ c". Native (and the Java property spec)
treats `\\` as always-decoded to a single backslash regardless
of the useJavaEscapes flag. Tracked separately as a bridge
escape-decoding bug.

bridge default config treats `{n}` placeholders as inline
codes (Okapi's default code-finder rules tag them and strip
them from the source text), yielding "Text1". Native passes
them through as plain text. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior. Could also be reconciled by disabling
code-finder in the bridge config.

Bridge transport gap — same as escaped_quotes. Native decodes
\\n → LF and \\t → TAB inside captured text.

Bridge transport gap — bridge emits zero Blocks without a
rule list. Native decodes \\\" to a literal quote.

Bridge transport gap — bridge emits zero Blocks without a
rule list. Native decodes "" → ".

Bridge transport gap — the rules list isn't passable through
flat FilterParams, so the bridge emits zero Blocks. Native
extracts the two entries with their ids.
Bridge transport gap — same as section_with_two_keys.

Bridge transport gap — bridge emits zero Blocks without a
rule list. Native extracts the two key=value pairs and
discards the section header / comments as Data.
Bridge transport gap — same as single_entry.

Bridge transport gap — the okf_regex rules list is not
expressible through gRPC FilterParams (map<string,string>),
so the bridge runs with its default empty rules and emits
zero Blocks. Native correctly extracts the entry. Tracked
as a transport-level limitation, not an Okapi semantic
divergence — see the file header.

Bridge transport gap — same as single_entry. Native extracts
three Blocks; bridge emits zero.

Bridge transport gap — bridge emits zero Blocks without rules.
Native picks up `title=Hello`, `LABEL "World"`, `desc=Goodbye`
via the two rules in order.

Bridge transport gap — bridge emits zero Blocks without a
rule list. Native extracts the entry; the note is asserted
by the native unit test (regex.TestNoteExtraction) rather
than by the BlockTexts assertion vocabulary used here.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native decodes literal-
character escapes inline. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native decodes \'HH escapes
inline. Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native decodes \uN escapes
inline. Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF; the LayerStart/LayerEnd
scaffolding still emits but no Block surfaces between
them. Native generic RTF reader emits one Block per \par.
Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF input. Native generic RTF
reader emits one Block per paragraph with inline formatting
collapsed. Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native skips the color table
and emits the paragraph. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF; native generic RTF reader
extracts the paragraph and skips the font table. Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native skips the info group
and emits the paragraph. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native skips the stylesheet
and emits the paragraph. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter expects Trados-
tagged RTF; the plain testdata fixture yields 0 blocks.
Native generic RTF reader emits one Block per \par. Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter is the Trados-
tagged RTF filter and only extracts content wrapped in
Trados translation tags; plain/generic RTF produces 0 blocks.
Native is a generic RTF reader that emits a Block per
\par-terminated run. Both legitimate; spec asserts native
(generic-RTF) behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native decodes special-char
control words to their Unicode equivalents inline. Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi RTFFilter (Trados-tagged)
yields 0 blocks for plain RTF. Native decodes smart-quote
control words inline. Both legitimate; spec asserts native
behavior.

bridge LF-collapse divergence (#536) — bridge consumes both
the splicer and the LF, emitting "Hello world"; native
preserves the LF, emitting "Hello \nworld". Spec asserts
native (LF-preserved) semantics.

bridge LF-collapse divergence (#536) — bridge consumes both
the splicer and the LFs, emitting "Line1 Line2 Line3"; native
preserves them. Spec asserts native (LF-preserved) semantics.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter keeps the raw
`\%` escape sequence in the extracted Block ("Price is 5\%
off."); native decodes it to a literal `%` ("Price is 5%
off."). Both legitimate; spec asserts native (decode
backslash-percent to a literal `%`) behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter folds the
display math into a Code on the surrounding TextUnit;
native flushes the paragraph at the math boundary and
yields two Blocks. Spec asserts native segmentation.

bridge feature difference — same as inline_math_in_paragraph:
Okapi TEXFilter folds the display math into a Code on the
surrounding TextUnit, native flushes the paragraph at the
math boundary. Spec asserts native segmentation.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter keeps the
surrounding paragraph as one translatable TextUnit with
the math span collapsed into an inline Code, while native
flushes the paragraph at the math boundary and yields two
Blocks ("Energy is" and "here."). Both honor the
contract that the math source itself is non-translatable;
they disagree on whether the surrounding text is one
Block or two. Spec asserts native segmentation.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter does not skip
the verbatim environment in its default configuration: it
concatenates the verbatim content with the following
paragraph, yielding 2 Blocks ("Before.", "verbatim content
hereAfter."). Native skips the entire \begin{verbatim}…
\end{verbatim} as Data, yielding 2 clean Blocks ("Before.",
"After."). Spec asserts native (verbatim is non-translatable
and isolated from surrounding paragraphs) behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter collapses the
soft line break inside a paragraph, yielding "Second
textThird text"; native preserves it as "Second text\nThird
text". Both yield 2 Blocks. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native (preserve source line breaks inside a paragraph)
behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter does not extract
the section text when an optional [short] argument precedes
the brace argument: the bridge yields only the body
paragraph ("Body text."), dropping "Long Section Title"
entirely. Native correctly skips the optional argument and
extracts the full title. Both yield 1 Block from the
surrounding body; native yields 2 total Blocks (heading +
body), bridge yields 1. Spec asserts native behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter's default
configuration extracts \section/\subsection sections but
omits \subsubsection and \chapter from the paragraph-text
command list, so the bridge yields 3 Blocks ("Section",
"Subsection", "Chapter") while native yields 4 ("Section",
"Subsection", "Subsubsection", "Chapter"). Spec asserts
native (full LaTeX2e sectioning hierarchy is translatable)
behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter handles backslash
escapes differently: it drops the `\$` sequence entirely
(yielding "Price is 10" — the dollar sign and the literal
escape both lost) and keeps `\%` as the raw escape sequence
("5\% off"), giving "Price is 10 and 5\% off.". Native
decodes both escapes to their literal characters, giving
"Price is $10 and 5% off.". Spec asserts native (decode
backslash-escapes to literal characters) behavior.

bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter drops the `~`
(LaTeX non-breaking space) without substitution, yielding
"Wordone is here." (no separating space). Native substitutes
a regular space, yielding "Word one is here.". Spec asserts
native (decode ~ to a regular space in extracted text)
behavior.
bridge error: drain stream: daemon: The source language specified is 'en', but no <tuv> in the <tu> are set to this language.
bridge error: drain stream: daemon: The source language specified is 'en', but no <tuv> in the <tu> are set to this language.
bridge error: drain stream: daemon: The source language specified is 'en', but no <tuv> in the <tu> are set to this language.
bridge error: drain stream: daemon: The source language specified is 'en', but no <tuv> in the <tu> are set to this language.

Same numerus-stream divergence as `two_forms` — Okapi emits
one TU per numerusform (2 translatable units), native emits
one Block. The bridge also strips `%n` from the source via
its default code-finder, so its first block text is " file(s)"
rather than "%n file(s)". Spec asserts the native view.

Two divergences from the spec on the bridge side:
  1. Okapi TsFilter emits one StartGroup followed by one TU
     per `<numerusform>` (so two translatable text units for
     an en→fr two-form numerus message), while the native
     reader emits a single Block carrying both forms in
     numerusform:N properties. Both representations are
     legitimate; the divergence is in the part-stream shape.
  2. Okapi's default code-finder rules (regex from the
     Default Inline Code Pattern) strip `%n` from the source
     text yielding " file(s)" instead of "%n file(s)". The
     native reader treats `%n` as literal text under the
     default config. Both legitimate.
Spec asserts the native single-Block / literal-source view.

bridge feature difference — even with `escapeBrMode=false` the Okapi TTMLFilter emits `
` as a structured XLIFF inline code (an XML/XLIFF tag like ``) rather than the literal text `
`. Native preserves the source-side string `
` verbatim inside the Block. Both honor the spec contract that `
` becomes part of the translatable surface; they disagree on the in-Block string representation. Spec asserts native (literal `
` string) behavior.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
and produces 0 translatable TextUnits. Native wraps the
extracted Block in a Layer scoped to ttx / application/
x-ttx+xml. Tracked separately under #453 / #486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
and extracts no text. Native strips the inline tag elements
inside <Seg> and emits the joined character data. Tracked
separately under #453 / #486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
and merges TUs into one TextUnit. Native produces one Block
per <Tu> with the MatchPercent attribute on each. Tracked
separately under #453 / #486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
inside the segmented `<Tu>` so segmentMode=2 only surfaces
the unsegmented runs (joined into one TextUnit). Native
correctly emits one Block per segmented TU plus one Block
per unsegmented run. Tracked separately under #453 / #486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
so the segmentMode AUTO branch never sees the segments
either. Native correctly extracts the segmented content
while skipping unsegmented whitespace. Tracked separately
under #453 / #486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
and produces 0 translatable TextUnits. Native correctly
extracts only the segmented content under segmentMode=1.
The bridge's composite schema also rejects the flat
`segmentMode` key with "Unknown property" — the native
reader's flat key matches the schema's `x-flattenPath`
while the bridge's schema validator expects the grouped
`mode.segmentMode` path. Tracked separately under #453 /
#486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
as a segment-content wrapper and produces 0 translatable
TextUnits for the canonical Trados Studio TTX shape.
Native extracts one Block per <Tu>. Tracked separately
under #453 (parity divergence) and #486 (root-cause bug).

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
and produces 0 translatable TextUnits. Native extracts the
lone TUV as a source-only Block. Tracked separately under
#453 / #486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
and additionally merges every <Tu> within one <Raw> into a
single multi-segment TextUnit. Native extracts one Block
per <Tu>. Tracked separately under #453 / #486.

bridge bug #486 — Okapi TTXFilter does not recognise <Seg>
and extracts no text. Native decodes the XML entities to
their literal characters in the Block source. Tracked
separately under #453 / #486.

bridge daemon emits 0 translatable Blocks from inline
packageBody snippets (it opens cleanly and walks the part
stream but does not recognise the importContentInstance
pair through the document-open path). Tracked in #453's
okf_vignette row as the remaining bridge-side gap; the
native side passes the contract since the #501 rewrite.

bridge daemon extracts 0 Blocks from Test01.xml — the
fixture parses cleanly through the document-open path but
the importContentInstance pairs are not surfaced as
translatable content (same upstream limitation as the
inline snippet examples). Native side extracts at least
one Block since the #501 rewrite.

bridge daemon emits 0 translatable Blocks from inline
packageBody snippets (same root cause as
bilingual_pair::simple_doc_one_pair); native side passes.

bridge daemon emits 0 translatable Blocks from inline
packageBody snippets (same root cause as
bilingual_pair::simple_doc_one_pair); native side passes.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter defaults to mergeCaptions=true and
merges the two cues into a single block because the first cue
text lacks sentence-terminating punctuation. Native emits one
Block per cue per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter defaults to mergeCaptions=true and
joins intra-cue lines with spaces, so the two chapter cues
collapse to a single block whose text has spaces where newlines
should be. Native emits one Block per cue with line endings
preserved per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter defaults to mergeCaptions=true and
joins intra-cue lines with spaces, so the two cues with
align/line settings collapse to a single block. Native emits
one Block per cue with line endings preserved per spec
semantics. Tracked separately.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines, so the non-empty cue's text
reads with a space where the original "\n" should be. Native
preserves line endings per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines, so quoted multi-line cues
read with a space where the original newline should be.
Native preserves line endings per spec semantics. Tracked
separately.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines. Markup passes through but
"\n" inside <v> spans collapses to a space. Native preserves
line endings per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
("Line one Line two Line three.") rather than preserving the
original newlines. Native keeps literal "\n" between lines per
spec semantics. Tracked separately.

bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines, so each block's text reads
"Thanks everyone for joining us today." instead of carrying
a literal "\n" between lines. Native preserves line endings
per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

native bug — wiki reader does not honour the DokuWiki
`<file>` / `<code>` block tags, so it surfaces the entire
line `"This is <file> a test."` as a single Block. Bridge
correctly truncates extraction at `<file>` and emits only
`"This is"`. Tracked as a native bug (DokuWiki block-tag
recognition; tracked under #523).
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently
okapi-testdata not found at /tmp/wt-wiki/okapi-testdata — run scripts/fetch-okapi-testdata.sh
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

bridge segments at finer granularity than native — Okapi
XLIFF2Filter exposes only the first segment's text as the
Block's accessible source ("Sample segment."), keeping the
second segment ("Segment's content.") as a separate
internal segment that doesn't surface to has_block_with_text.
Native concatenates all segments into the joined Block
source. Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge default config emits both translate="yes" and
translate="no" units as Blocks (counting 2 blocks, with
"Do not translate" present), since the assertion-level
view does not yet honor the non-translatable flag. Native
excludes translate="no" units from the translatable Block
list. Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge default config does not expose ignoreInputSegmentation
(Okapi XLIFFFilter rejects the property as Unknown), so the
bridge falls back to using <source> verbatim and yields
"Sentence one. Sentence two." (with the inter-segment space).
Native discards the segmentation and concatenates the seg-source
mrk contents directly to "Sentence one.Sentence two.". Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge does not accept the camelCase `excludeByDefault` key
— it expects `exclude_by_default` (with underscores) per the
Okapi YAML parameter naming convention. Without the flag
the bridge falls back to default-include policy and
surfaces every <item>'s text. Native ApplyMap accepts the
camelCase spelling. Both legitimate; spec asserts native
behavior. A matching variant of this example using the
underscore key is left for a future authoring pass once
the spec runner supports per-side config aliases.

bridge expects `parser.preserveWhitespace=true` (or
`preserve_whitespace: true` in YAML), not a top-level
`preserveWhitespace` boolean — the bridge composite schema
flattens the parser group via x-flattenPath but the daemon
does not synthesise the parser wrapper from the flat
spelling, so the bridge falls back to the default collapse
rule. Native ApplyMap accepts the flat spelling as a
convenience. Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge does not recognise the top-level `translatableElements`
array — Okapi XmlStreamFilter expects the equivalent rule
to be declared as `elements: { title: { ruleTypes: [TEXTUNIT] }, description: { ruleTypes: [TEXTUNIT] } }`,
so the bridge surfaces all three text nodes (title, id,
description) as Blocks. Native treats the shorthand as a
first-class extraction filter. Both legitimate; spec
asserts native behavior.

bridge segments anchor/alias references at coarser
granularity than native — Okapi YmlFilter exposes the
anchored value once (1 block) instead of emitting at
each use site. Native walks the alias target node directly
(yaml.v3 Node.Alias) and yields one block per occurrence.
Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

bridge segments flow-style mappings at finer granularity
than native — Okapi YmlFilter extracts both the string and
the numeric scalar (yielding 2 blocks with "John Smith" and
"33"). Native skips non-string scalars under default config
and yields 1 block. Both legitimate; spec asserts native
behavior.

bridge bug — folded (`>`) scalar should join continuation
lines with a space and keep the trailing newline ("Joined
text\n"). Bridge yields "Joined text" with no trailing
newline. Native matches YAML 1.2 folded-scalar semantics.
Tracked separately.

bridge bug — Okapi YmlFilter loses newlines in literal
(`|`) block scalars, joining "Line 1\nLine 2\n" into
"Line 1Line 2". Native preserves newlines per YAML 1.2
literal-scalar semantics. Tracked separately as a bridge
multiline-block-scalar handling bug.

bridge bug — same multiline-block-scalar newline loss as
literal_block. Bridge yields "This is avery long line.";
native preserves the embedded newline ("This is a\nvery
long line.") per YAML 1.2 strip-chomp semantics. Tracked
separately.

bridge default config treats `{{count}}` as inline code
(Okapi's default code-finder rules tag and strip it from
the source text), yielding "My  Rails Website". Native
passes mustache placeholders through as plain text. Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.
bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently
